article.title: Topical Spray of dsRNA Induces Mortality and Inhibits Chilli Leaf Curl Virus Transmission by Bemisia tabaci Asia II 1

events.event_label: dsRNA targeting hsp70 induces mortality and reduces ChiLCV transmission
events.evidence_context_text: PMC8909865.p051.s009: RNAi using dsRNA constructs is not suitable for field conditions due to rapid degradation and a lack of reliable delivery agents. PMC8909865.p051.s010: In the present study, for the first time, we demonstrated the efficacy of a naked dsRNA against an insect pest by spray-on application under semi-field conditions. PMC8909865.p051.s011: The dsRNA construct targeting the hsp70 gene of B. tabaci not only induced mortality in B. tabaci but also inhibited the transmission of ChiLCV from infected to healthy chilli plants by B. tabaci.

events.event_label: dsRNA treatment eliminates ChiLCV copies and blocks transmission
events.evidence_context_text: PMC8909865.p053.s006: The resistance to ChiLCV in B. tabaci was enhanced at higher concentrations of hsp70 dsRNA. PMC8909865.p053.s007: ChiLCV copies in B. tabaci were imperceptible when B. tabaci fed on 3 μg/mL hsp70 dsRNA for 24 h. The transmission of ChiLCV from infected to healthy plants by B. tabaci Asia II 1 was completely inhibited at 3 μg/mL hsp70 dsRNA treatment under controlled conditions. PMC8909865.p053.s008: Our findings are consistent with Kanakala et al., who reported the deleterious effect of B. tabaci hsp70 dsRNA on TYLCV transmission. PMC8909865.p053.s009: The TYLCV titer decreased in B. tabaci Middle East Asia Minor 1 (MEAM 1) after feeding on hsp70 dsRNA-expressing tomato plants. PMC8909865.p053.s010: Transmission of TYLCV also dropped by 12% post-silencing of hsp70.

events.event_label: dsRNA lowers ChiLCV copy number and transmission
events.evidence_context_text: PMC8909865.p054.s012: A 67.77% mortality with a 4.6-fold decrease in hsp70 mRNA levels in B. tabaci exposed to the dsRNA-sprayed plants was noted after 24 h of release. PMC8909865.p054.s013: Further, 1.84 × 108-fold decreased ChiLCV copies and 75% reduced transmission of ChiLCV indicated the efficiency of topically sprayed hsp70 dsRNA in managing both the virus and its vector. PMC8909865.p054.s014: Although the efficacy of topically applied naked dsRNA against plant viruses is known, this is the first evidence of its efficacy against a hemipteran insect like B. tabaci.

events.event_label: dsRNA treatment affects B. tabaci mortality and ChiLCV transmission

PMC8909865.p051.s010: In the present study, for the first time, we demonstrated the efficacy of a naked dsRNA against an insect pest by spray-on application under semi-field conditions. PMC8909865.p051.s011: The dsRNA construct targeting the hsp70 gene of B. tabaci not only induced mortality in B. tabaci but also inhibited the transmission of ChiLCV from infected to healthy chilli plants by B. tabaci.

events.event_label: Silencing hsp70 and fas2 affects mortality and virus transmission
events.evidence_context_text: PMC8909865.p006.s003: The objective of the present study was to induce resistance against B. tabaci and ChiLCV by spray-on application of dsRNA. PMC8909865.p006.s004: In the present study, B. tabaci hsp70 and fas2 were silenced and resultant effects in mortality and virus transmission were reported. PMC8909865.p006.s005: For the first time, the efficacy of spray-on application of naked dsRNA against a hemipteran insect was demonstrated under semi-field conditions. PMC8909865.p006.s006: The outcomes of the study provide a novel, eco-friendly option for protection against B. tabaci as well as ChiLCV, which can reduce economic losses caused by the virus-vector complex.

events.event_label: hsp70 produces siRNA
events.evidence_context_text: PMC8909865.p037.s001: A conserved 128 nt-long (2416 to 2543 nt) fragment of the hsp70 gene (~2.5 kb) and a 150 nt-long (1085 to 1234 nt) fragment of the fas2 gene (~1.4 kb) of B. tabaci were selected for dsRNA designing (Figure 1a,b). PMC8909865.p037.s002: In siRNA Wizard 3.1, the 128 nt-long B. tabaci hsp70 fragment produced a putative siRNA of 21 nt (5’-GAUCCAUCCAUGCCGUUAAUC-3’). PMC8909865.p037.s003: The 150 nt-long fas2 fragment also yielded one putative siRNA of 21 nt (5’-GGACGGAAGAAGGCAUAAUCA-3’). PMC8909865.p037.s004: The dsRNA sequence was specific to B. tabaci, and no cross-reactivity was recorded with other organisms such as Homo sapiens, Mus musculus, Aves (taxid:8782), Lepidoptera (taxid:7088), Hymenoptera (taxid:7399), Formicidae (taxid:36668), and plants (taxid:3193) in BLAST analysis.

events.event_label: Higher temperature and UV radiation degrade dsRNA
events.evidence_context_text: PMC8909865.p054.s001: Although RNAi has emerged as a promising alternative to suppress crop pests, there are limitations in the application of dsRNA as a ‘spray-on’ technology for field use. PMC8909865.p054.s002: Reduced stability in the extracellular environments of dsRNA contributes to poor RNAi response. PMC8909865.p054.s003: The naked dsRNA quickly degrades in higher temperatures and UV radiation under natural environmental conditions. PMC8909865.p054.s004: Besides, several insect species, mainly lepidopteran insects, have been observed to be refractory to RNAi due to degradation and poor intracellular transport of exogenous dsRNA.

events.event_label: dsRNA application increases B. tabaci mortality and inhibits ChiLCV transmission

PMC8909865.p054.s011: Considering the extracellular degradation of dsRNA and delivery through the host plant, a 10-fold higher dose than the controlled assay was used for topical application. PMC8909865.p054.s012: A 67.77% mortality with a 4.6-fold decrease in hsp70 mRNA levels in B. tabaci exposed to the dsRNA-sprayed plants was noted after 24 h of release. PMC8909865.p054.s013: Further, 1.84 × 108-fold decreased ChiLCV copies and 75% reduced transmission of ChiLCV indicated the efficiency of topically sprayed hsp70 dsRNA in managing both the virus and its vector.

events.event_label: dsRNA of hsp70 and fas2 were produced
events.evidence_context_text: PMC8909865.p037.s006: The fragments were cloned between two T7 promoters of L4440 RNAi vector (Addgene 1654 provided by Andrew Fire, Carnegie Institution for Science, Washington, DC, USA). PMC8909865.p037.s007: The recombinant plasmids were transformed into RNase III mutant Escherichia coli HT115 cells (provided by Caenorhabditis Genetics Center, Minneapolis, MN, USA). PMC8909865.p037.s009: The purified dsRNA of hsp70 and fas2 produced single specific bands of ~130 bp and ~150 bp on 2% native agarose gel (Figure 1c). PMC8909865.p037.s010: Upon bidirectional sequencing of the recombinant plasmids, both the sequences of hsp70 (Accession No. MZ158306) and fas2 (Accession No. MZ766125) showed 100% homology with B. tabaci hsp70 and fas2, respectively.

events.event_label: fas2 dsRNA and water treatments affect ChiLCV copy number
events.evidence_context_text: PMC8909865.p042.s009: The virus copies were undetectable in B. tabaci with a further increase in hsp70 dsRNA concentration to 3.0 μg/mL. The retention of ChiLCV might be completely ceased at a higher concentration of hsp70 dsRNA or beyond the detection limit using qPCR. PMC8909865.p042.s010: In contrast, the ChiLCV copy number acquired by fas2 dsRNA-treated B. tabaci was significantly higher than the untreated control (Figure 3d). PMC8909865.p042.s011: When B. tabaci was fed with sterile distilled water instead of fas2 dsRNA, the mean ChiLCV copy number was 3.19 × 1011, whereas the virus copies increased significantly by 139.18-fold (4.44 × 1013 copies) at 1.0 μg/mL of fas2 dsRNA feeding. PMC8909865.p042.s012: However, the mean virus copies were estimated to be 3.02 × 1013 and 2.14 × 1013 after exposure to 2.0 and 3.0 μg/mL fas2 dsRNA, respectively. PMC8909865.p042.s013: Although significantly higher numbers of virus copies were recorded in all fas2 dsRNA doses compared to the control, higher doses of fas2 dsRNA recorded a decline in virus copies compared to the lower doses.

events.event_label: dsRNA treatment increases B. tabaci mortality
events.evidence_context_text: PMC8909865.p052.s015: Similar types of artificial feeding setup were reported to be efficient in the oral delivery of dsRNA. PMC8909865.p052.s016: A significant knockdown of the B. tabaci population was recorded at 24 h, which gradually increased with an increase in dsRNA concentration. PMC8909865.p052.s017: Up to 82.22% mortality of B. tabaci was recorded after 24 h feeding on 3.0 μg/mL hsp70 dsRNA. PMC8909865.p052.s018: Similarly, fas2 dsRNA at 3.0 μg/mL caused 72% mortality of B. tabaci 24 h post-feeding. PMC8909865.p052.s019: The dsRNA exposure downregulated the hsp70 and fas2 mRNA expressions by 12.85- and 8.53-fold, respectively.

events.event_label: Midgut proteins Hsp, cyclophilins and peptidoglycan recognition protein interact with begomovirus coat protein
events.evidence_context_text: PMC8909865.p005.s015: Interrupting the interrelationship between B. tabaci and begomovirus using RNAi is a promising approach.. For successful persistent-circulative transmission, the begomovirus particles need to cross the midgut barrier of B. tabaci to circulate in hemolymph and reach the primary salivary glands. PMC8909865.p005.s016: Several proteins at the midguts of B. tabaci, such as heat shock proteins (Hsp), cyclophilins, peptidoglycan recognition protein, and a midgut protein, are known to interact with begomovirus coat protein (CP) for successful internalization. PMC8909865.p005.s017: RNA-Seq of B. tabaci in response to begomoviruses revealed significant upregulations of hsp70, fasciclin 2 (fas2), and several other transcripts. PMC8909865.p005.s018: In gene regulatory network analyses, these genes were enriched with higher degrees of interactions. PMC8909865.p005.s019: The same trend was recorded in the mRNA expression in reverse transcriptase quantitative real-time PCR (RT-qPCR) post-exposure to ChiLCV.

Although RNAi has emerged as a promising alternative to suppress crop pests, there are limitations in the application of dsRNA as a ‘spray-on’ technology for field use.
Reduced stability in the extracellular environments of dsRNA contributes to poor RNAi response.
The naked dsRNA quickly degrades in higher temperatures and UV radiation under natural environmental conditions.
Besides, several insect species, mainly lepidopteran insects, have been observed to be refractory to RNAi due to degradation and poor intracellular transport of exogenous dsRNA.
Nucleases are the major factors limiting the efficacy of dsRNA.
